content.rule: There are situations where no agent can proceed without explicit approval from the owner, no matter how obvious the next step looks. When you hit any trigger, stop, document the situation, present options, and wait.
content.why: Actions taken without an answer can generate lost work or a real problem. Each instance is new — the owner approving a push yesterday does not grant permission today.
how_to_apply: Spending money: paid API calls, cloud deploys, domain/license purchases, ad spend — anything that shows up on the owner's invoice
External publication: git push to main/master on public repo, merging PR, social media post, email to mailing list, app store release, publishing a live page
Destructive action: rm -rf, git reset --hard, git push --force on shared branch, drop table, mass delete, revoking credentials, deleting issue/PR/branch with history, overwriting uncommitted content
Structural change: creating new specialist/manager, adding/modifying core rule, changing recorded decision, architecture change outside task scope, altering agent frontmatter

Unresolvable ambiguity: contradiction between rules, task affecting domains where managers would disagree, strategic (what) not tactical (how) decision, any situation requiring inference of owner intent

examples.situation: Task requires calling an image generation API that costs $0.04 per call
examples.right: Escalate: spending money trigger. Present the cost estimate and ask for approval

examples.situation: Found that git push --force would fix the branch issue quickly
examples.wrong: Force push to fix the branch
examples.right: Escalate: destructive action trigger. Present alternatives (revert commit, new branch, etc.)

anti_patterns: Asking permission for everything — escalation is for this list only, not every micro-step
Skipping a trigger because the owner already approved something similar before — each instance is new
Escalating without options — always present at least 2 options, even if one is 'do nothing'
Starting work while waiting for an answer on a structural decision — almost always becomes rework
content.responsibility: All agents without exception, including specialists created via Hiring Loop. Leo has special role: retroactively reviews whether any trigger should have fired during execution.
